question: Unit vs integration vs e2e tests (the testing pyramid).
answer:
• Unit — one class/function in isolation, dependencies mocked. Fast, many.
  • Integration — several units together (a component + its template + a real child).
  • E2E — the whole app in a browser, simulating a real user. Slow, few.

The pyramid: lots of unit tests, fewer integration, fewest e2e.
tip: Test behaviour, not implementation — so tests survive refactors.
deep:
The pyramid

        /\        E2E        few    — slow, brittle, but REAL confidence
       /  \       (10%)
      /----\      Integration       — components + template + DI
     /      \     (20%)
    /--------\    Unit              many — fast, precise, cheap
   /__________\   (70%)
UnitIntegrationE2E
Testsone class in isolationa component + its templatethe real app in a browser
Speedmilliseconds~100msseconds
Depsall mockedsome realnothing mocked
Failure tells youexactly what brokeroughly"something broke"
Confidence it works for a userlowmediumhigh

The trade-off the shape encodes

Unit tests are fast and pinpoint the break, but passing them doesn't mean the app works — you mocked the parts that fail in production. E2E proves it genuinely works but is slow, flaky, and tells you nothing about where it broke. So: many cheap tests, few expensive ones.

The two anti-patterns — name them

  • Ice-cream cone (inverted): mostly E2E, few unit. CI takes 40 minutes, tests fail randomly, and people start re-running until green — at which point the suite has negative value, because it's training the team to ignore failures.
  • Hourglass: lots of unit + lots of E2E, no integration. Every unit passes, the app is broken, because nothing tested the wiring.

The modern pushback (worth raising)

Kent C. Dodds' "testing trophy" argues integration deserves the biggest slice, not unit — because most real bugs live in the wiring between units, not inside them. A component test that renders the template and asserts what the user sees catches more real bugs per line than a test asserting an internal method was called.

In Angular terms: a TestBed test that renders the component and checks the DOM usually beats a test that instantiates the class and pokes at fields. Mentioning this shows you've thought about testing rather than memorised a diagram.

What to test at each level

  • Unit — pure logic: services, pipes, reducers, selectors, validators. Cheap and high value.
  • Integration — a component with its template: does clicking emit? does the list render?
  • E2E — a handful of critical revenue paths only: login, checkout, submit. Not every edge case.

How to say it in an interview

“Many fast unit tests, fewer integration, and a handful of E2E on the critical paths — because unit tests pinpoint the break but mock away what actually fails, and E2E proves it works but is slow and tells you nothing about where. The failure mode I've seen is the inverted cone: an E2E-heavy suite that's flaky, so people re-run until it's green and the suite stops meaning anything. I lean a bit toward the testing-trophy view — in Angular most real bugs are in the wiring, so component tests that render the template earn their keep.”

question: What are Jasmine and Karma in Angular?
answer: Jasmine is the testing framework — describe/it/expect with matchers and spies. Karma is the test runner that launches real browsers and reports results. (Newer Angular is moving to Jest/Vitest + web-test-runner.)
tip: Structure tests AAA: Arrange, Act, Assert.

question: What is TestBed and configureTestingModule?
answer: TestBed creates an Angular testing module — a mini environment where you declare/import the component under test and provide (usually mocked) dependencies. TestBed.createComponent() then gives you a ComponentFixture.
tip: Override real services with mocks in the providers array here.
deep:
What it is

TestBed creates a miniature Angular module for the test — its own injector, compiler, and change-detection context. It's how you test a component with its template and DI rather than as a bare class.

describe('UserComponent', () => {
  let fixture: ComponentFixture<UserComponent>;

  beforeEach(async () => {
    await TestBed.configureTestingModule({
      imports: [UserComponent],                    // standalone: import it
      providers: [{ provide: UserService, useValue: mockService }]  // swap real deps
    }).compileComponents();

    fixture = TestBed.createComponent(UserComponent);
    fixture.detectChanges();                       // triggers ngOnInit + first render
  });
});

declarations vs imports

The change that catches people out: standalone components go in imports, not declarations. declarations is for NgModule-based components only — put a standalone one there and you get "Component X is standalone, and cannot be declared in an NgModule".

Overriding dependencies — the whole point

providers: [
  { provide: UserService, useValue: jasmine.createSpyObj('UserService', ['getUsers']) },
  { provide: ActivatedRoute, useValue: { snapshot: { params: { id: '1' } } } }
]

This is why DI matters: because the component asks for UserService rather than constructing it, the test can substitute a fake. That's the payoff of dependency inversion — a great link to make if they ask "why DI?"

The mistake that silently breaks tests

// ✘ resolves the REAL service — the override is ignored
const svc = new UserService();

// ✔ pulls from the TestBed injector — gets your mock
const svc = TestBed.inject(UserService);

Also: TestBed.overrideProvider() must be called before createComponent(). After that the injector is built and overrides are ignored — silently.

Why it's slow, and the escape hatch

TestBed compiles templates — that's real work, and it's why component suites are far slower than plain unit tests. For a service, pipe, or reducer with no template, skip it entirely:

// no TestBed needed — it's just a class
const service = new UserService(mockHttp);
expect(service.parse(raw)).toEqual(expected);

Knowing when not to use TestBed is as valuable as knowing how to configure it.

Isolation

Angular resets the TestBed between specs automatically, so state doesn't leak. compileComponents() is only needed with external templateUrl files and no build-time inlining — with the modern CLI it's usually optional.

How to say it in an interview

“It builds a mini Angular module for the test — its own injector and change detection — so I can render a component with its template and swap real dependencies for fakes via providers. That's dependency inversion paying off. The gotchas: standalone components go in imports not declarations, you must use TestBed.inject rather than new or you get the real service, and overrides have to happen before createComponent. For services and pipes I skip TestBed entirely — plain new is much faster.”

question: ComponentFixture — detectChanges, nativeElement, debugElement?
answer: The fixture wraps the component + its DOM. detectChanges() runs change detection (nothing renders until you call it). nativeElement is the raw DOM; debugElement is Angular's wrapper with query(By.css()) for finding elements.
tip: Forgetting fixture.detectChanges() is the #1 reason a template assertion fails.
deep:
What the fixture gives you

const fixture = TestBed.createComponent(UserComponent);

fixture.componentInstance   // the class — call methods, set @Inputs
fixture.nativeElement       // the real DOM element
fixture.debugElement        // Angular's DOM wrapper (queries, injector)
fixture.detectChanges()     // RUN change detection
await fixture.whenStable()  // wait for pending async

detectChanges() is the thing people forget

component.name = 'Ann';
expect(fixture.nativeElement.textContent).toContain('Ann');   // ✘ FAILS
// The class changed. The DOM didn't — nothing told Angular to re-render.

component.name = 'Ann';
fixture.detectChanges();                                       // ✔ now it renders
expect(fixture.nativeElement.textContent).toContain('Ann');

In a real app, Zone.js triggers change detection automatically after events. In tests it does not — you drive it manually. That's the #1 cause of "my test fails but the app works."

The first detectChanges() is also what triggers ngOnInit. Assert before it and lifecycle hooks haven't run yet — which is occasionally exactly what you want to test.

nativeElement vs debugElement

nativeElementdebugElement
Isthe real DOM nodeAngular's wrapper
QueryquerySelector('.x')query(By.css('.x'))
Find by directive✘By.directive(MyDir) ✔
Get the child's injector✘.injector.get() ✔
Component instance of a child✘.componentInstance ✔
// debugElement can reach INTO a child component
const child = fixture.debugElement.query(By.directive(ChildComponent));
expect(child.componentInstance.value).toBe(42);

// triggerEventHandler fires Angular's binding, not a raw DOM event
fixture.debugElement.query(By.css('button'))
  .triggerEventHandler('click', null);

Rule of thumb: nativeElement for asserting what the user sees (text, classes, attributes); debugElement when you need Angular's knowledge — directives, child components, the injector.

autoDetectChanges — and why not to

fixture.autoDetectChanges();   // CD runs automatically

Convenient, but you lose the ability to assert intermediate state, and it can hide bugs where your component depends on an extra CD cycle. Most teams keep it manual and explicit.

OnPush needs special care

With ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush, mutating a component field won't re-render even with detectChanges() — OnPush only reacts to input reference changes, events, or markForCheck. Set a new input reference via fixture.componentRef.setInput() (Angular 14.1+), which correctly marks the component dirty.

How to say it in an interview

“The fixture wraps the component: componentInstance for the class, nativeElement for the DOM, debugElement for Angular-aware queries. The key thing is that change detection doesn't run automatically in tests — Zone triggers it in the app, but in a spec I call detectChanges(), and the first call is what fires ngOnInit. I use nativeElement to assert what the user sees and debugElement when I need to reach a child component or fire an Angular binding. With OnPush I set inputs through componentRef.setInput so it's actually marked dirty.”

question: How do spies work (spyOn, createSpy, returnValue)?
answer: A spy records calls and can fake return values. spyOn(obj, 'method') replaces a real method; jasmine.createSpyObj builds a whole mock. Assert with toHaveBeenCalledWith.
tip: createSpyObj is the clean way to mock a service with several methods.
deep:
The three ways to make one

// 1. spyOn — wrap an EXISTING method on a real object
spyOn(service, 'getUsers').and.returnValue(of([mockUser]));

// 2. createSpy — a standalone fake function
const cb = jasmine.createSpy('callback');

// 3. createSpyObj — a whole fake object (the usual choice for DI)
const mock = jasmine.createSpyObj<UserService>('UserService', ['getUsers', 'save']);
mock.getUsers.and.returnValue(of([]));

TestBed.configureTestingModule({
  providers: [{ provide: UserService, useValue: mock }]
});

Controlling behaviour

spy.and.returnValue(of([]));            // fixed value
spy.and.returnValues(of([a]), of([b])); // different per call
spy.and.callThrough();                  // spy, but still run the real thing
spy.and.callFake(id => of({ id }));     // custom implementation
spy.and.throwError('boom');             // simulate failure
spy.and.resolveTo(user);                // async

Asserting

expect(spy).toHaveBeenCalled();
expect(spy).toHaveBeenCalledWith('123');
expect(spy).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);
expect(spy).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
expect(spy.calls.mostRecent().args[0]).toBe('123');
expect(spy.calls.count()).toBe(2);

The trap: returning a raw object where an Observable is expected

// ✘ component does .subscribe() -> TypeError: ...subscribe is not a function
mock.getUsers.and.returnValue([mockUser]);

// ✔ match the real signature
mock.getUsers.and.returnValue(of([mockUser]));

A spy doesn't check the return type at runtime — createSpyObj<UserService> gives you some compile-time help, but an untyped spy will happily return nonsense and fail deep inside the component.

The real risk: spies let you assert the wrong thing

// ✘ testing the IMPLEMENTATION — passes even if the UI is broken
expect(service.getUsers).toHaveBeenCalled();

// ✔ testing the BEHAVIOUR — the user sees users
expect(fixture.nativeElement.querySelectorAll('.user-row').length).toBe(2);

Asserting "the method was called" couples the test to how the code works. Rename or refactor the method and the test breaks despite nothing being wrong for the user — and conversely, it passes while the template is broken. Verify a call when the call is the outcome (an analytics event, a save request); otherwise assert what the user gets.

Over-mocking

Mock everything and you test your mocks, not your code. The suite goes green while production is broken, because the mock returns a shape the real API never sends. Mock the boundary (HTTP, time, randomness), not your own internals.

Jest note

Same ideas, different names: jest.fn(), jest.spyOn(), mockReturnValue(), toHaveBeenCalledWith().

How to say it in an interview

“createSpyObj for a whole injected dependency, spyOn to stub one method on a real object, and and.returnValue/callFake to control it. The trap is returning a raw array where the component expects an Observable — the spy won't stop you, it just fails inside the component. The bigger point is what I assert: toHaveBeenCalled tests implementation and breaks on refactors, so I only verify the call when the call is the outcome, like a save request. Otherwise I assert what the user actually sees.”

question: How do you test HttpClient calls?
answer: Import HttpClientTestingModule and inject HttpTestingController. It intercepts requests so no real network happens: expectOne(url) asserts the request, flush(data) returns a fake response, and verify() ensures nothing is outstanding.
tip: Call httpMock.verify() in afterEach to catch unexpected or missing requests.
deep:
The pattern

describe('UserService', () => {
  let service: UserService;
  let httpMock: HttpTestingController;

  beforeEach(() => {
    TestBed.configureTestingModule({
      imports: [HttpClientTestingModule],   // replaces HttpClient's backend
      providers: [UserService]
    });
    service  = TestBed.inject(UserService);
    httpMock = TestBed.inject(HttpTestingController);
  });

  afterEach(() => httpMock.verify());   // ← fails if any request went unhandled

  it('fetches users', () => {
    let result: User[] | undefined;
    service.getUsers().subscribe(users => result = users);   // 1. subscribe FIRST

    const req = httpMock.expectOne('/api/users');            // 2. assert the request
    expect(req.request.method).toBe('GET');
    req.flush([{ id: '1', name: 'Ann' }]);                   // 3. respond

    expect(result!.length).toBe(1);                          // 4. assert the result
  });
});

HttpClientTestingModule swaps the real backend for a mock one, so no network call happens — the test is fast and deterministic.

Why you must subscribe first

HttpClient observables are cold — the request isn't issued until something subscribes. Call expectOne before subscribing and it fails with "Expected one matching request, found none". This is the cold-observable concept showing up in a test, and it's a nice connection to draw.

verify() is the bit people skip

httpMock.verify() in afterEach asserts no unexpected requests were made. Without it, a stray duplicate call (say two async pipes triggering two GETs) passes silently. It's how the test suite catches the accidental-double-request bug.

Testing the error path

it('handles a 500', () => {
  let error: unknown;
  service.getUsers().subscribe({ error: e => error = e });

  httpMock.expectOne('/api/users').flush(
    'Server error',
    { status: 500, statusText: 'Internal Server Error' }
  );

  expect(error).toBeTruthy();
});

// network failure (not an HTTP status)
req.error(new ProgressEvent('network error'));

Note the two are different: flush(body, { status }) is a server response with an error code; req.error() is a transport failure. Knowing the distinction is a good detail.

Matching more precisely

httpMock.expectOne(r => r.url === '/api/users' && r.params.get('page') === '2');
httpMock.expectNone('/api/users');        // assert it did NOT fire (e.g. cached)
const reqs = httpMock.match('/api/users'); // expect several

Modern note

HttpClientTestingModule is deprecated in Angular 17.3+ in favour of provideHttpClientTesting() alongside provideHttpClient() — same concepts, functional providers.

How to say it in an interview

“HttpClientTestingModule — now provideHttpClientTesting — swaps the backend so nothing hits the network. Subscribe first, because HTTP observables are cold and the request won't exist otherwise, then expectOne, assert the method and body, and flush a response. The part people skip is verify() in afterEach, which fails the test on any unexpected request — that's what catches an accidental duplicate call. For errors, flush with a 500 for a server error, or req.error() for a network failure.”

question: fakeAsync + tick vs async + whenStable.
answer: fakeAsync runs a test in a virtual time zone; tick(ms) advances timers synchronously and flush() drains all pending ones — ideal for setTimeout/debounce. async/whenStable waits for real async tasks (promises) to settle.
tip: Use fakeAsync + tick to test debounced search without real waiting.
deep:
The problem

Async code doesn't finish before your assertions run. You could use a real setTimeout and a done() callback — but then a debounceTime(500) test genuinely takes 500ms, and 50 of those add 25 seconds to CI. And it's flaky.

fakeAsync — virtual time

it('debounces the search', fakeAsync(() => {
  component.search('ang');
  expect(service.search).not.toHaveBeenCalled();   // nothing yet

  tick(300);                    // fast-forward 300ms INSTANTLY
  expect(service.search).toHaveBeenCalledWith('ang');

  flush();                      // drain anything still pending
}));

Zone.js patches the timer APIs, so setTimeout, setInterval and Promises are queued rather than executed. tick(300) advances a fake clock and runs whatever was scheduled — in microseconds of real time, and deterministically.

The toolkit

CallDoes
tick(ms)advance the virtual clock by ms
tick()flush microtasks (promises) only
flush()run all pending macrotasks until the queue is empty
flushMicrotasks()promises only
discardPeriodicTasks()drop pending setIntervals

The error everyone hits

// Error: 1 periodic timer(s) still in the queue.

fakeAsync fails the test if timers are still pending at the end — deliberately, because a leaked interval is usually a real bug. Fix it properly (the component should clean up in ngOnDestroy) or call discardPeriodicTasks(). Reaching for discardPeriodicTasks without asking why a timer leaked is how you paper over a genuine leak.

What fakeAsync CANNOT do

// ✘ XHR is NOT patched by fakeAsync
it('loads', fakeAsync(() => {
  service.getFromRealHttp().subscribe();
  tick();   // does nothing — a real network call isn't a timer
}));

That's why HTTP tests use HttpTestingController and flush() the response, not tick().

fakeAsync vs waitForAsync

fakeAsync + tickwaitForAsync + whenStable
Timevirtual — instantreal
Controlprecise (tick(299) vs tick(300))none — wait for everything
Determinismtotalcan be flaky
Use fortimers, debounce, most thingstemplateUrl compilation, real async

async() was renamed waitForAsync in Angular 11 (it collided with the JS keyword). Prefer fakeAsync: it's faster and it lets you assert the state between ticks, which is where debounce bugs hide.

The future: zoneless

fakeAsync depends on Zone.js. As Angular moves zoneless, expect more reliance on TestScheduler marble tests and await fixture.whenStable().

How to say it in an interview

“fakeAsync gives you virtual time — Zone.js patches the timers so tick(300) fast-forwards instantly and deterministically, which means a debounce test runs in microseconds instead of 300ms and never flakes. The nice part is asserting between ticks: nothing called at 299ms, called at 300. It errors if timers are still queued, which usually means a real leak rather than something to discard. It doesn't patch XHR, so HTTP tests use HttpTestingController instead.”

question: How do you test an observable?
answer: Subscribe and assert inside the callback. For synchronous streams (of) assertions run immediately; for async use fakeAsync/done. Verify emitted values and completion.
tip: For simple cases, subscribe and assert; for timing, reach for marble tests.
deep:
Synchronous streams: just subscribe

it('maps users', () => {
  let result: string[] | undefined;
  service.getNames().subscribe(names => result = names);   // of() emits SYNCHRONOUSLY
  expect(result).toEqual(['Ann']);      // already ran — no async needed
});

If the source is of() or a BehaviorSubject, the subscribe callback runs immediately. No done, no fakeAsync.

The silent-pass bug

// ✘ If the observable NEVER emits, this test PASSES — with zero assertions
it('loads', () => {
  service.getUsers().subscribe(users => {
    expect(users.length).toBe(2);   // never runs. Test is green. 🎉💀
  });
});

An assertion inside a callback that never fires is an assertion that never runs — and Jasmine reports success. This is the single most dangerous observable-testing mistake. Two fixes:

// 1. assign outside, assert outside
let result;
obs$.subscribe(v => result = v);
expect(result).toEqual(expected);   // fails if it never emitted

// 2. or count your assertions
expect().nothing();   // explicit, if genuinely no assertion

Async streams — three tools

// A. done callback — simple, but a timeout on failure
it('loads', (done) => {
  service.getUsers().subscribe(users => {
    expect(users.length).toBe(2);
    done();                          // forget this -> 5s timeout
  });
});

// B. fakeAsync — for timers/debounce
it('debounces', fakeAsync(() => {
  let v; obs$.pipe(debounceTime(300)).subscribe(x => v = x);
  subject.next('a');
  tick(300);
  expect(v).toBe('a');
}));

// C. async/await via firstValueFrom — cleanest for one-shot streams
it('loads', async () => {
  const users = await firstValueFrom(service.getUsers());
  expect(users.length).toBe(2);
});

C is the modern default for a single emission — no done to forget, reads linearly.

Asserting a sequence of emissions

it('emits loading then data', () => {
  const emitted: State[] = [];
  service.state$.subscribe(s => emitted.push(s));   // collect

  service.load();
  expect(emitted).toEqual([
    { status: 'idle' },
    { status: 'loading' },
    { status: 'success' }
  ]);
});

Collecting into an array is the pragmatic way to assert order without marble syntax.

Don't forget errors and completion

obs$.subscribe({
  next:     v => values.push(v),
  error:    e => errorSeen = e,
  complete: () => completed = true
});

A stream that errors when you expected data will silently do nothing if you only passed a next handler.

How to say it in an interview

“If it's synchronous — of() or a BehaviorSubject — I just subscribe and assert. For a single async emission I'd await firstValueFrom, which avoids the done callback entirely. For timers or debounce, fakeAsync with tick. The mistake I watch for is putting the assertion inside the subscribe callback — if the observable never emits, the callback never runs, no assertion executes, and the test passes green. So I assign outside and assert outside.”

question: What is marble testing?
answer: A way to test RxJS timing using ASCII 'marble diagrams' with the TestScheduler. Characters represent virtual time: - a frame, letters emissions, | complete, # error. Great for operators like debounceTime and switchMap.
tip: Marble tests make time-based operator behaviour precise and readable.
deep:
The syntax

'--a--b--|'    // emit 'a' at 20ms, 'b' at 50ms, complete at 80ms
'--a--#'       // emit 'a', then ERROR at 50ms
'--a--b--'     // never completes
'(ab|)'        // a and b SAME frame, then complete
'^--a--b'      // ^ = subscription point (hot observables only)
CharMeans
-1 frame (10ms of virtual time)
a-zan emission
|complete
#error
()same-frame grouping
^subscription point

A real test

it('debounces', () => {
  const scheduler = new TestScheduler((actual, expected) =>
    expect(actual).toEqual(expected));

  scheduler.run(({ cold, expectObservable }) => {
    const source$ = cold('  -a--b--c---|');
    const expected = '      ------b--(c|)';   // 'a' dropped — 'b' came too soon after
    expectObservable(source$.pipe(debounceTime(30, scheduler))).toBe(expected);
  });
});

Inside scheduler.run(), RxJS operators automatically use the virtual scheduler — so debounceTime(30) costs zero real milliseconds and can never flake.

Why bother when fakeAsync exists?

marblefakeAsync
Assertsthe whole emission timelinestate at chosen points
Timingdeclarative, visualimperative tick()
Tests errors/completionnaturally (#, |)awkwardly
Zone.js needednoyes
Learning curvereallow

Marble tests shine for operator-heavy logic — a complex NgRx effect, a custom operator, a race between streams — because the expectation is a picture of the timeline. And being Zone-independent, they survive the move to zoneless Angular.

Asserting subscriptions — the killer feature

scheduler.run(({ cold, expectObservable, expectSubscriptions }) => {
  const source$ = cold('  -a--b--c--|');
  const subs =  '         ^------!';     // subscribed at 10ms, UNSUBSCRIBED at 70ms

  expectObservable(source$.pipe(take(2))).toBe('-a--(b|)');
  expectSubscriptions(source$.subscriptions).toBe(subs);
});

This proves cancellation happened — that switchMap really did unsubscribe from the stale inner request. No other technique asserts that directly, which is why it's the definitive answer to "prove switchMap cancels."

cold vs hot

  • cold('--a--b|') — starts when subscribed. HTTP-like.
  • hot('--a--^--b|') — already running; ^ marks where you subscribe, so a is missed. Subject-like.

The honest framing

Most teams don't write many marble tests — the syntax is dense and hard to debug when it fails. They're worth it for genuinely timing-dependent logic. Saying "I use them for complex effects and custom operators, not for everything" is more credible than claiming they're your default.

How to say it in an interview

“ASCII diagrams of a stream over virtual time — - is a frame, letters are emissions, | completes, # errors — run through TestScheduler, so a 300ms debounce test is instant and deterministic. The expectation is literally a picture of the timeline, which is great for operator-heavy code. The feature I actually value is expectSubscriptions — it proves an unsubscribe happened, so it's the only clean way to assert that switchMap really cancelled the stale request. I use them for complex effects, not everywhere.”

question: How do you unit-test a service?
answer: Services are plain classes — instantiate directly or via TestBed.inject(), mock their dependencies, call methods, and assert results/side effects. No fixture/DOM needed unless the service touches HTTP.
tip: Services are the easiest and highest-ROI things to unit-test — start there.

question: How do you test a pipe?
answer: Pipes are pure functions — instantiate the class and assert transform() output for various inputs. No TestBed required.
tip: Pipe tests are trivial — just new the pipe and check transform().
code:
const pipe = new TruncatePipe();
expect(pipe.transform('hello world', 5)).toBe('hello…');

question: How do you test a directive?
answer: Create a tiny host component whose template uses the directive, render it via TestBed, then assert the directive's effect on the host element (classes, styles, events).
tip: The host-component pattern is the standard way to test attribute directives.

question: How do you test @Input and @Output?
answer: Input: set the property on component, call detectChanges(), assert the rendered result. Output: subscribe to the EventEmitter (or spy on it), trigger the action, and assert it emitted the expected payload.
tip: Trigger a click via debugElement.triggerEventHandler to test outputs realistically.

question: What are CDK Component Harnesses?
answer: From @angular/cdk/testing: a harness is a reusable API for interacting with a component in tests without depending on its DOM structure — so tests don't break when internal markup changes. Angular Material ships harnesses for its components.
tip: Harnesses decouple tests from DOM internals — more robust than querying CSS.
deep:
The problem harnesses solve

// ✘ Reaching into Angular Material's INTERNAL DOM
const input = fixture.nativeElement.querySelector('.mat-mdc-input-element');
input.value = 'Ann';
input.dispatchEvent(new Event('input'));

You've just coupled your test to Material's private markup. Material 15 migrated to MDC and renamed essentially every internal class — .mat-input-element became .mat-mdc-input-element, and thousands of tests across the ecosystem broke at once. Nothing was wrong with anyone's app.

The harness API

const loader = TestbedHarnessEnvironment.loader(fixture);

const input = await loader.getHarness(MatInputHarness.with({ placeholder: 'Name' }));
await input.setValue('Ann');

const button = await loader.getHarness(MatButtonHarness.with({ text: 'Save' }));
await button.click();

const rows = await loader.getAllHarnesses(MatRowHarness);
expect(rows.length).toBe(3);

You interact through a supported public API — setValue, click, getText. Material can restructure its DOM freely; the harness adapts and your test doesn't change.

Why every call is awaited

Harness methods are all async, and deliberately so: each one handles change detection and stabilisation internally. That's why harness tests rarely need manual detectChanges() — the harness does it. Forgetting an await is the usual bug, and it fails confusingly.

The same test runs in unit AND e2e

// unit (TestBed)
const loader = TestbedHarnessEnvironment.loader(fixture);

// e2e (Protractor/Selenium environment)
const loader = ProtractorHarnessEnvironment.loader();

// ...identical harness code below this line

This is the underrated part: the environment is swappable, so the same harness interactions work at both levels. It's the strongest argument for the abstraction.

You can write your own

export class UserCardHarness extends ComponentHarness {
  static hostSelector = 'app-user-card';

  private getName = this.locatorFor('.user-name');
  async getUserName(): Promise<string> {
    return (await this.getName()).text();
  }
}

Harnesses aren't Material-only — publishing one alongside a shared component in a design system means consumers test against your API rather than your DOM. That's the mature use, and worth mentioning.

When not to bother

For a simple component with no Material dependency, nativeElement plus data-testid is less ceremony. Harnesses earn their keep with component libraries — anything whose internal DOM isn't yours to depend on.

How to say it in an interview

“They're a supported API for interacting with a component in tests instead of querying its internal DOM — await input.setValue('Ann') rather than finding .mat-mdc-input-element and dispatching an input event. The reason it matters: when Material moved to MDC it renamed all its internal classes and broke everyone's selector-based tests, while harness-based tests kept passing. Every method is async because the harness handles change detection for you, and the same harness code runs in both unit and e2e environments. I'd also write custom ones for shared design-system components.”

question: How do you test routing/navigation?
answer: Provide the router in test mode (RouterTestingModule or provideRouter([])), spy on Router.navigate, or use a RouterTestingHarness to drive real navigation and assert the activated component.
tip: Spying on router.navigate is the quick way to assert a guard/redirect fired.

question: What is code coverage and is 100% the goal?
answer: Coverage measures which lines/branches your tests execute (ng test --code-coverage). It shows untested code but not test quality — 100% coverage of trivial getters proves little. Aim for meaningful coverage of logic and edge cases, not a vanity number.
tip: High coverage of the wrong things is a false sense of safety — prioritise critical paths.
deep:
What the four numbers mean

MetricMeasures
Statementslines executed
Brancheseach if/else, ternary, && path taken
Functionsfunctions called
Lines≈ statements

Branch coverage is the one that matters. Statement coverage is easy to inflate; branches force you through the else nobody tested.

ng test --code-coverage    # -> coverage/index.html

Why 100% is not the goal

function divide(a: number, b: number) { return a / b; }

it('divides', () => expect(divide(10, 2)).toBe(5));
// 100% statement, branch, function and line coverage.
// divide(10, 0) -> Infinity. Completely untested. Ship it. 🚀

Coverage measures execution, not verification. It tells you a line ran, never that you asserted anything meaningful about it. You can hit 100% with zero assertions:

it('covers everything', () => {
  component.doEverything();   // no expect() at all
});                            // -> green, and coverage goes up

The right way to read it

Low coverage is a reliable signal; high coverage is not. 20% tells you something real is untested. 95% tells you almost nothing about quality — but the uncovered 5% is a genuinely useful list of what you forgot.

So use it as a finder of gaps, not a grade.

Goodhart's law — the real danger

"When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." Mandate 100% and you get:

  • Tests written for the metric — no assertions, just execution.
  • Tests on trivial getters and the CLI's "should create", inflating the number.
  • /* istanbul ignore next */ sprinkled to dodge hard cases — the exact code most worth testing.

A team at 100% with assertion-free tests is worse off than one at 70% with sharp tests, because they believe they're safe.

What a sensible policy looks like

  • ~80% as a guardrail, not a trophy — and enforce it on changed lines (diff coverage), so new code is tested without a big-bang backfill.
  • Near-100% on pure logic: reducers, selectors, validators, pipes, money math. Cheap and high value.
  • Lower on wiring, config, and generated code.
  • Judge by critical paths covered, not the global percentage.
// angular.json — a floor that stops regressions
"codeCoverage": { "thresholds": { "statements": 80, "branches": 75 } }

The better metric

Mutation testing (Stryker) mutates your code — flips a > to >=, removes a line — and checks whether a test fails. If nothing goes red, the line was executed but never verified. It measures assertion quality, which is what people think coverage measures. Raising this is a strong signal.

How to say it in an interview

“It's the percentage of code executed by tests — branch coverage being the meaningful one. But it measures execution, not verification: a test with no assertions still counts, and divide(10, 2) gives 100% while divide(10, 0) is untested. So low coverage is a real signal and high coverage isn't — I use the uncovered list to find gaps rather than chasing a number. Mandating 100% just gets you assertion-free tests and ignore comments. I'd set ~80% on changed lines as a guardrail, push near-100% on pure logic like reducers, and if I really wanted to know test quality I'd run mutation testing.”

question: E2E options: Cypress / Playwright vs Protractor.
answer: Protractor was Angular's original e2e tool — now deprecated. Modern choices are Cypress (great DX, time-travel debugging) and Playwright (multi-browser, fast, auto-wait). They drive the real app and assert user-visible behaviour.
tip: Mention that Protractor is deprecated — recommend Cypress or Playwright.
deep:
Protractor is dead

Deprecated and removed from Angular 16+. It relied on Selenium/WebDriver and Angular-specific waiting (waitForAngular), which broke as the ecosystem moved on. If you say "we use Protractor", expect a follow-up about migration — the answer is Cypress or Playwright.

Cypress vs Playwright

CypressPlaywright
Runsinside the browserout-of-process (CDP)
BrowsersChromium, Firefox, WebKitall three, better parity
Multi-tab / multi-originhistorically painfulnative
Parallelismpaid dashboard (or DIY)free, built in
LanguageJS/TSTS, Python, Java, .NET
Debuggingexcellent time-travel UItrace viewer (also excellent)
Auto-wait✔✔

Cypress's in-browser architecture is its strength (great DX, time-travel) and its weakness (tabs, iframes, multiple origins fight it). Playwright has become the default for new projects — free parallelism and multi-origin support matter in CI. Both are massive upgrades over Protractor.

The thing that makes E2E bearable: auto-waiting

// Selenium/Protractor era — sleep and pray
browser.sleep(2000);
element(by.css('.user')).click();

// Modern — retries until it exists, is visible, and is actionable
cy.get('.user').click();
await page.click('.user');

Hard-coded sleeps are the classic flakiness source: too short and it fails on a slow CI box, too long and the suite crawls. Auto-waiting retries against the assertion instead.

Selectors decide your flake rate

// ✘ breaks when a designer changes the layout or copy
cy.get('.btn.btn-primary.mt-3');
cy.contains('Submit');

// ✔ a contract that exists only for tests
cy.get('[data-testid="submit-order"]');
await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit order' });   // also good — accessible

data-testid (or a role-based query) decouples the test from styling. Playwright's getByRole has a bonus: if the query can't find it by role, a screen reader probably can't either — so the test doubles as an accessibility check.

What to put in E2E — and what not to

  • Do: a handful of critical revenue paths — login, checkout, submit. The things that cost money when broken.
  • Don't: every validation permutation. Test those at unit level, where they run in 2ms instead of 20s.

The failure mode is a 200-test E2E suite taking 45 minutes and failing randomly, so the team re-runs until green — at which point it has negative value.

Determinism

Seed or mock the backend (cy.intercept / page.route), reset state between specs, and never depend on test order or on data another test created. A shared mutable database is how E2E suites become haunted.

How to say it in an interview

“Protractor's deprecated and gone from Angular 16, so it's Cypress or Playwright — I'd pick Playwright for new work, mainly for free parallelism and proper multi-origin and multi-tab support; Cypress has the nicer in-browser debugging but its architecture fights those cases. Both auto-wait, which kills the sleep(2000) flakiness. I select with data-testid or roles so restyling doesn't break tests, and I keep E2E to a few critical paths — a 45-minute flaky suite that people re-run until green is worse than no suite.”

question: What makes a good unit test?
answer:
• Tests behaviour, not implementation details.
  • Fast, isolated, and deterministic (no real network/time).
  • One logical assertion/concept per test; descriptive names.
  • Arrange-Act-Assert structure; mock external dependencies.
tip: If a refactor that keeps behaviour identical breaks your test, the test was too coupled.
deep:
The one rule everything follows: test behaviour, not implementation

// ✘ implementation — breaks on rename, passes while the UI is broken
expect(component.loadUsers).toHaveBeenCalled();
expect(component['_internalCache'].size).toBe(2);

// ✔ behaviour — survives refactors, fails when the user is affected
expect(fixture.nativeElement.querySelectorAll('.user-row').length).toBe(2);

The test for a good test: if I refactor the internals without changing what the user sees, does it still pass? If no, it's coupled to implementation and will cost you more than it earns — it turns every refactor into test-fixing busywork and trains people to distrust the suite.

AAA — and one assertion concept per test

it('shows an error when the save fails', () => {
  // Arrange
  mockService.save.and.returnValue(throwError(() => new Error('boom')));
  // Act
  component.save();
  fixture.detectChanges();
  // Assert
  expect(fixture.nativeElement.querySelector('.error').textContent)
    .toContain('Could not save');
});

"One assertion per test" is often misquoted — it's one concept. Three expects verifying one behaviour is fine; three unrelated behaviours in one it is not, because the first failure hides the rest.

Name the test as a sentence

// ✘ tells you nothing when it goes red in CI
it('works', ...)
it('test user service', ...)

// ✔ the failure output IS the bug report
it('disables the submit button while the form is invalid', ...)
it('retries the request twice before showing an error', ...)

The FIRST properties

  • Fast — a slow suite doesn't get run.
  • Isolated — no shared state; order must not matter. Tests that pass alone and fail in suite are almost always leaked state in a beforeEach.
  • Repeatable — no reliance on real dates, randomness, network, or timezone. new Date() in a test is a time bomb that fails at midnight or in another timezone — inject a clock.
  • Self-validating — passes or fails; no reading console output.
  • Timely — written with the code, not "later".

Don't test the framework

// ✘ this tests Angular, not your code
it('creates', () => expect(component).toBeTruthy());

The CLI-generated "should create" spec is nearly worthless — it only proves the constructor didn't throw. Keep it as a smoke test if you like, but it isn't coverage.

The trap: a test that can't fail

Write the assertion, then deliberately break the code and confirm the test goes red. A test you've never seen fail is a test you don't know works — this is where mutation testing gets its argument. Assertions inside an observable callback that never fires are the classic silent-pass.

How to say it in an interview

“Test behaviour, not implementation — the check is whether I can refactor internals without touching the test. Asserting toHaveBeenCalled on my own methods couples the test to structure, so it breaks on refactors and still passes when the template's broken. Beyond that: AAA structure, names that read as a sentence so the CI failure is the bug report, no real dates or randomness, and no shared state between specs. And I make sure I've seen each test fail — a test that can't fail is worse than none, because it buys false confidence.”
